coredata.dc:description: The development of high-efficiency photocatalysts for water splitting to hydrogen (H2) is crucial for alleviating energy crisis and environmental problems. In this study, a novel type-II ternary heterojunction photocatalyst, CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, was fabricated through the integration of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) with Cu2O and carbon dots (CDs), exhibited excellent photocatalytic H2 production rate of 12817.1 μmol/g/h with apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 17.13 % under visible light irradiation. Characterization revealed a uniform and dense structural integration with a high Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 275.9 m2 g−1 and a suitable energy band gap of 2.18 eV. Furthermore, the heterostructures promoted efficient photo-generated carrier separation and enhanced reduction capabilities. Notably, CDs, serving as cocatalysts or electron acceptors, improved visible light utilization and electron migration, significantly boosting H2 production. Insights from electron spin resonance (ESR) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations supported the migration mechanisms of electrons. This work presents a significant advance in photocatalytic technology for sustainable energy by designing high-performance CDs-modified heterojunction photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as electron acceptors modified NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O heterojunction for highly efficient photocatalytic H2 production

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-efficiency photocatalysts for water splitting to hydrogen (H2) is crucial for alleviating energy crisis and environmental problems. In this study, a novel type-II ternary heterojunction photocatalyst, CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, was fabricated through the integration of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) with Cu2O and carbon dots (CDs), exhibited excellent photocatalytic H2 production rate of 12817.1 μmol/g/h with apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) of 17.13 % under visible light irradiation. Characterization revealed a uniform and dense structural integration with a high Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of 275.9 m2 g−1 and a suitable energy band gap of 2.18 eV. Furthermore, the heterostructures promoted efficient photo-generated carrier separation and enhanced reduction capabilities. Notably, CDs, serving as cocatalysts or electron acceptors, improved visible light utilization and electron migration, significantly boosting H2 production. Insights from electron spin resonance (ESR) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations supported the migration mechanisms of electrons. This work presents a significant advance in photocatalytic technology for sustainable energy by designing high-performance CDs-modified heterojunction photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: As society progresses, the challenges of energy and environmental sustainability have become increasingly complex. Hydrogen (H2) energy, recognized for its high energy density, substantial calorific value, and zero carbon emissions [1,2], is a promising alternative to fossil fuels. Moreover, H2 is an essential feedstock in process such as hydrogenation reactions, petroleum refining, and fertilizer production [3–5]. Water splitting methods, including thermal catalysis [6,7], electrocatalysis [8,9], and photocatalysis [10], have emerged as viable approaches for H2 production. Among these, photocatalysis offers a promising route to convert solar energy into H2, a green and renewable fuel [11–13]. Currently, the development and optimization of efficient photocatalysts are pivotal. NH2-MIL-125 is recognized for its potential in photocatalytic water splitting due to its environmental compatibility, adjustable electronic structure, and robust redox capabilities [14]. It possesses a suitable band gap (2.6 eV) and excels in visible light absorption [15,16]. However, its solo use is limited by the rapid recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes.

To enhance photocatalytic efficiency, various strategies have been explored, including morphological manipulation [17], doping with precious metals [18], dye sensitization [19], and heterostructure fabrication [20]. Among these, forming heterojunctions stands out as an effective, economical, and environmentally friendly approach, significantly enhancing photostability and corrosion resistance. Cu2O, a semiconductor with a bandgap of 2.0–2.2 eV, is notable for its excellent response in the visible spectrum [21] and its non-toxic, low-cost, and straightforward synthetic attributes. The staggered energy band arrangement at the heterojunction interface promotes type-II electron transfer, enhancing photocatalytic activity [22]. For example, Xu et al. [23] prepared Cu2O/NH2-MIL-125 heterocomposite using a simple solution method via the reaction between NH2-MIL-125 and Cu(NO3)2 aqueous solution under room temperature. Compared with NH2-MIL-125, the Cr (VI) degradation efficiency of Cu2O/NH2-MIL-125 photocatalyst is about 1.5 times that of NH2-MIL-125. The tight contact interface and type-II heterostructure endow the photocatalyst with high carrier transport and separation efficiency, thus improving the photocatalytic performance. However, the construction of heterojunctions still faces difficulties in separating photo-generated charges, and catalytic performance needs to be further improved.

Previous research has highlighted the role of carbon-based materials as electron acceptors or cocatalysts in boosting the activity of heterogeneous photocatalysts [24]. Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have gained popularity due to their biocompatibility, low toxicity, and superb water solubility [25–27]. In addition, CDs have exceptional photoelectrochemical properties, including good up-conversion photoluminescence, electrical conductivity, and a wide range of light absorption capacities [28,29]. These properties make CDs ideal cocatalysts, enhancing the photocatalytic activity by facilitating the separation of photogenerated carriers and optimizing the utilization of visible light [30]. Meng et al. [31] prepared CDs/CdS@MIL-101 heterojunction composites by introducing CDs and CdS QDs (quantum dots) into porous MIL-101 pores by a one-step double solvents method and heat treatment. CDs/CdS@MIL-101 can effectively promote the photocatalytic H2 production performance. The successful fabrication of heterostructures reduces the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs and accelerates the separation of photogenerated carriers. CDs as a cocatalyst can change the band structure of the composite photocatalysts and improve the utilization efficiency of visible light. Therefore, developing excellent CDs-modified heterogeneous photocatalysts not only helps to improve H2 production performance, but also has important significance in alleviating energy shortages.

In this work, we introduce a novel ternary heterostructure, CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, synthesized through a simple electrostatic self-assembly method using biomass-derived citric acid as a carbon source. This advanced composite photocatalyst significantly outperforms NH2-MIL-125 and Cu2O alone in H2 production under visible light. Furthermore, the photoelectrochemical characterization and density functional theory (DFT) calculations confirm the successful formation of the heterojunction and provide insights into the efficient electron transfer mechanisms within the ternary system. This development not only marks a significant step in improving H2 production efficiencies but also contributes broadly to addressing global energy challenges.

Triethanolamine (TEOA, 99.0 %), Tetra-n-butyl titanate (Ti(OC4H9)4, ≥98.0 %), and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5 %) were bought from Shanghai Ling Feng Co., Ltd. 2-amino-1,4-benzenedicarboxylic acid (H2bdc-NH2, >98.0 %) and ascorbic acid (C6H8O6, >99.0 %) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd., China. Methanol (CH3OH, ≥99.5 %), ammonia (NH3 ·H2O, 25 %–28 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96.0 %), and absolute ethyl alcohol (CH3CH2OH, ≥99.7 %) were obtained from National medicine group Co., Ltd., China. Copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2 ·2H2O, 99 %), citric acid (C6H8O7, 98 %), and Eosin Y (C20H6Br4Na2O5, 80 %) were obtained from Meryer Co., Ltd., China. All the chemicals were used without any further purified.

NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) was synthesized by solvothermal method [32]. In a typical experiment, a certain amount of H2bdc-NH2 and Ti(OC4H9)4 were mixed with a solution containing DMF and CH3OH. The above mixture was stirred until it was uniform. Then the mixed solution was placed in a reaction kettle and reacted at a certain temperature for 8 h. After the reaction, the obtained sample was washed several times with anhydrous DMF and CH3OH, respectively. The product NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) was obtained after drying at 80 °C in final.

Cu2O was synthesized by liquid-phase reduction according to the existing reports [33]. First, 200 mL of NaOH (2 mol/L) was slowly added to a round-bottomed flask containing 200 mL of CuCl2 (0.05 mol/L) and stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 mL of ascorbic acid (0.6 mol/L) was added to the above mixture and continued stirring for 1 h until the color changed from sky blue to dark red, then the solution was aged overnight in the dark. Next, the obtained dark red powder was washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol twice, respectively. Finally, the product Cu2O was acquired after drying in vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h.

NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O was prepared by the electrostatic self-assembly method. First, anhydrous ethanol was dropped into Cu2O and stirred for several hours until evenly dispersed. Then, a certain amount of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) was added to the above mixture and continued to stir for 6 h. The above solution was evaporated to a small amount of solvent at room temperature and was dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)n/Cu2O binary heterojunction photocatalyst. The prepared sample is labeled NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)n/Cu2O (n = 10, 30, 60, 70), where n represents the mass (mg) of NH2-MIL-125 added.

CDs are produced by the hydrothermal method. First, citric acid (1.00 g) was dissolved in ammonia (10 mL) and then sonicated for 30 min. Then, the mixture solution was transferred into an autoclave and heated at 205 °C for 24 h. Finally, CDs were collected by filtering the final solution using a microporous PTFE membrane (50 mm, 0.22 μm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O ternary heterojunction photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O ternary heterojunction photocatalyst was prepared by the electrostatic self-assembly method (Fig. 1 ). First, the NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O photocatalyst was added to anhydrous ethanol (10 mL) and stirred for 1 h until evenly dispersed. Then, the CDs solution was put in the above-mixed solution, stirred for 6 h, and volatilized at room temperature until a small amount of solvent remained. Subsequently, the CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O ternary composite photocatalyst was obtained by vacuum drying at 60 °C for 12 h. The prepared sample was labeled as CDsn/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O (n = 0.5, 1, 2), where n represents the mass percentage of CDs in CDsn/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and density functional theory calculation
ce:section.ce:para: Detailed information of characterization and calculation is shown in the supporting information.

Photocatalytic H2 production experiments were carried out in a 180 mL quartz reactor. First, 10 mg of photocatalyst was added to the mixed solution containing 1 mL TEOA, 29 mL water, and 10 mg Eosin Y (EY). Subsequently, the mixture was stirred for 15 min, foamed with Ar to ensure that the reactor was filled with Ar and the air in the reactor was discharged. The photocatalytic activity was measured under a 300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cutoff filter (λ > 420 nm). The generated H2 was detected by gas chromatography (GC-9750, FULI) equipped with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).

Fig. 2 a presents the X-ray diffractometer (XRD) patterns of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) 60/Cu2O, and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) 60/Cu2O. Cu2O exhibits six distinct diffraction peaks at 29.6°, 36.4°, 42.3°, 61.3°, 73.5°, and 77.3°, which correspond to the (110), (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) crystal planes, respectively [33]. The integration of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) results in three new peaks at 7.65°, 9.87°, and 13.12° in the NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, corresponding to the (101), (002), and (211) crystal planes of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti). This indicates the successful loading of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) onto the Cu2O surface [34]. Notably, the characteristic peak for CDs is absent in CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, likely due to the low concentration and high dispersion of CDs. The chemical structure of the photocatalysts is further studied by the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum. Fig. 2b shows characteristic absorption peaks for CDs at 3439 cm−1 and 1644 cm−1, attributed to the tensile vibration of O–H and CO groups [35], respectively. This suggests the presence of hydroxyl/carboxyl groups in CDs. The peak at 1387 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O–C tensile vibration [36]. For Cu2O, the absorption peak at 635 cm−1 is identifies as the Cu–O stretching vibration. After loading NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and CDs onto Cu2O, new peaks emerge between 642 and 766 cm−1 and 1338-1571 cm−1 for NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O samples. There are attributed to the stretching vibration of the O–Ti–O and the tensile vibration of the –OH in NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), respectively [37,38], these observations provide clear evidence of the successful integration of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) onto the surface of Cu2O. Regarding the CDs, due to the small amount of CDs introduced, no distinct characteristic peaks of CDs were observed in the FT-IR spectrum of CDs1/NH2-MIL-125(Ti)60/Cu2O composite. However, as depicted in Fig. S1, the Raman spectra of CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, Cu2O, and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) reveal the presence of characteristic peaks not only from the Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) but also the distinctive D and G bands of CDs, thereby confirming the successful incorporation of CDs into the composite [39].

The morphology and microstructure of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O photocatalysts were examined using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM images in Fig. 3 a reveals that Cu2O synthesized via the liquid phase reduction method, displays a uniform spherical morphology with a diameter of about 1 μm. NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), prepared through hydrothermal synthesis, presents a uniform size of discs with an average size of 500 nm in diameter as shown in Fig. 3b. Fig. 3c demonstrates that the Cu2O morphology remains intact after addition of CDs and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), with CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O exhibiting a tightly bonded disc and spherical morphology. This indicates successful loading of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) onto the Cu2O surface and effective construction of heterojunction. Further investigation of the interface structure of the composite photocatalysts was conducted using TEM and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), as shown in Fig. 3d-p, it can be revealed that the close binding between CDs, Cu2O, and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti). The prepared CDs are highly dispersed with an average particle size of 4 nm (Fig. 3f). Although CDs were not visible in SEM image, TEM analysis in Fig. 3h identifies their uniformly dispersion on the NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) 60/Cu2O surface, evidenced by lattice stripe spacing of 0.245 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of CDs [40]. EDS analysis of CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O reveals the presence of C, N, O, Ti, Cu (Fig. 3k-p), indicating a high distribution of these elements and confirming the successful synthesis of the ternary composite photocatalyst.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to elucidate the composition and chemical states of photocatalysts. Fig. 4 a presents the full XPS spectrum of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, featuring peaks for C 1s, Ti 2p, O 1s, and Cu 2p. This spectrum confirms the simultaneous presence of CDs and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) in CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O composite, indicating successful integration. Significant shifts in binding energy, known to influence the migration direction of charge carriers within photocatalysts, were observed [41]. In Fig. 4b, the Ti 2p spectrum of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) shows peaks at 464.8 and 459.0 eV for Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 [42], respectively. These peaks shift to lower binding energies in both NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, suggesting an increased electron density on titanium and electron accumulation [43]. As shown in Fig. 4c, the Cu 2p spectrum of Cu2O, with characteristic peaks at 951.8 and 932.0 eV attributed to Cu+ [44]. Additional peaks at 954.0 and 934.2 eV indicate the presence of Cu2+, a result of surface micro-oxidation of Cu2O [45]. In the composite, these peaks shift to 952.8 and 933.4 eV, respectively, reflecting a decrease in electron density on Cu 2p, which is indicative of electron migration from Cu 2p. It further confirms the formation of heterojunction between Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d) splits into peaks at 529.9 and 531.8 eV, associated with metal-oxygen bond and hydroxyl groups [46], respectively. The peaks also shift to higher binding energies in the composite, mirroring the changes observed in the Cu 2p spectrum and suggesting electron migration from Cu2O to NH2-MIL-125 (Ti). The observed modifications in binding energies across Ti 2p, O 1s, and Cu 2p in the composite catalysts not only illustrate changes in the electronic structure but also confirm a strong interfacial interaction among CDs, Cu2O, and NH2-MIL-125. This interaction facilitates the electron transfer mechanism essential for enhancing the photocatalytic performance, as indicated by Fermi level equilibrium [40,47].

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions were measured at 77 K to characterize the pore structure and specific Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area of the photocatalysts. Fig. 5 displays the N2 adsorption-desorption curves and pore size distributions of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O. All catalysts exhibit type IV isotherms according to the IUPAC classification, indicative of mesoporous structures [33] (Fig. 5a). As detailed in Table S1, the CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O photocatalyst shows the highest specific surface area at 275.9 m2 g−1 and pore volume of 0.162 cm3 g−1. These increased values the redox ability of the photocatalysts. According to the pore size distribution curve shown in Fig. 5b, the pore sizes for all samples predominately range from 2 to 50 nm, confirming their mesoporous structure. Mesoporous materials, compared to microporous counterparts, feature larger pores that accommodate macromolecules, resist pore blockage, and enhance photocatalytic activity. Additionally, their structure facilitates increased visible light absorption and a greater photocatalyst-light contact area, promoting the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of photocatalytic H2 evolution performance
ce:section.ce:para: The H2 production abilities of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O photocatalysts were assessed under identical conditions using TEOA as the sacrificial agent and EY as the photosensitizer, shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig. S2. NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) displayed no H2 production activity, while Cu2O produced H2 at a lower rate. In contrast, the NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O binary heterojunction exhibited significantly enhanced photocatalytic performance, with a H2 production rate of 12817.1 μmol/g/h, which is 2.67 times higher than that of Cu2O alone (4793.8 μmol/g/h). This is attributed to the heterostructure facilitating the migration and separation of photogenerated carriers, further accelerating the redox reaction. However, when the amount of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) gradually increases, the H2 production performance of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)70/Cu2O decreases, which may be due to the excessive addition of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) covering the active site of the binary heterojunction photocatalyst, thus reducing the photocatalytic activity. CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and CDs/Cu2O (Fig. S3) have lower H2 evolution activity compared with NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, the reason maybe that the addition of CDs cannot form heterojunction active centers for catalytic reactions [1]. However, introduction of CDs into NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O initially led to an increase in activity. When the content of CDs is 1 wt%, the composite photocatalyst CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O showed the highest H2 evolution rate of 12817.1 μmol/g/h, which is 1.44 times that of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O (Fig. 6b). Conversely, further increase in CDs content to 2 wt% resulted in diminished activity, possibly due to the CDs obstructing active site and impairing light absorption. The superior activity of the ternary composite is largely due to the role of CDs as electron acceptors or cocatalysts, enhancing electron migration and promoting the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs. As shown in Table S2, we compared our work with the reported CDs-modified photocatalysts works to further demonstrate the excellent performance of the photocatalyst synthesized in our work. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) values of CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O with different content of CDs were measured at 420 nm, and the results were summarized in Table S3. Among them, CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O exhibits an AQE of up to 17.13 %. Stability and durability tests indicated that after five cycles, the photocatalytic performance of CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O decreased slightly due to photo-corrosion of cuprous oxide (Fig. 6c). Yet it maintained excellent reusability and photostability, confirmed by XRD, XPS spectra, and SEM analyses. The crystal structure of the CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O composite remained consistent before and after the reaction, as evidenced by the XRD analysis (Fig. 6d). By comparing the XPS spectra of the catalyst before and after the reaction (Figs. S4a–c), it is observed that there were no obvious alterations in the valence states of the elements and electronic structure. Furthermore, the SEM images of the composite photocatalyst post-reaction (Fig. S4d) indicate that there were no significant changes in the surface morphology of the catalyst, suggesting that the photocatalytic activity was not adversely affected. Based on the characterization results and discussion of the catalyst before and after the reaction, it can be concluded that the CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O photocatalyst we prepared exhibits excellent photocatalytic stability. In addition, Raman spectroscopy after reaction further verified the presence of CDs in the used CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O photocatalyst, as evidenced by the characteristic G and D bands in the Raman spectrum (Fig. S5a). The catalysts prepared were further subjected to thermogravimetric (TG) measurement (Fig. S5b). As the temperature increased, these photocatalysts all exhibited good thermal stability, especially the ternary composite CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, which only experienced about a 10 % weight loss at temperature below 300 °C.

To investigate the light absorption characteristics of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O, and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O photocatalysts, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) studies were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, Cu2O exhibits a main absorption peak at approximately 420 nm and an absorption edge near 650 nm, demonstrating its responsiveness in the visible light spectrum. The pure NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) shows an absorption edge around 550 nm, indicating good visible light absorption capabilities. The NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O photocatalyst displays an expanded light absorption edge of to about 750 nm compared to the single components (Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)). The addition of CDs further enhances this characteristic, with the absorption edge of CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O expanding to approximately 800 nm. This suggests that the combined effect of CDs and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) significantly improves the visible light absorption of Cu2O, potentially boosting photocatalytic H2 production [48–50]. Fig. 7b presents the Tauc plot, from which the energy band gap of the photocatalysts were derived using the equation αhv = A(hv-E g)1/2 [51]. The synthesized CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O has a much narrower band gap width (2.18 eV) than pure NH2-MIL-125 (2.65 eV) and Cu2O (2.38 eV). The reduction in band gap indicates a lower energy requirement for electron transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), enhancing electron-holes separation and indicating an expanded absorption range in the visible light region, as well as superior light absorption performance.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were tested to examine the charge separation efficiency [52]. When the photosensitizer EY dye molecules are excited by light, they transition to an excited state (EY1∗). The electron lifetime in this state is notably short, and without timely reaction, energy is lost as fluorescence and heat, diminishing the visible light utilization efficiency. The faster the electron quenching of the excited state of EY, the higher the steady-state fluorescence emission peak intensity shown in Fig. 8 a. Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) exhibit high photoluminescence intensity, indicating a propensity for carrier charge recombination. The integration of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) with Cu2O as NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O reduces this intensity, suggesting improved electron migration across the heterojunction. CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O displays the lowest emission peak, implying that CDs, functioning as cocatalysts or electron acceptors, effectively inhibits the recombination of photo-induced carrier, accelerate electron transfer from NH2-MIL125 (Ti) and Cu2O to CDs, enhancing their participation in the photocatalytic reaction. Next, time-resolved photoluminescence (TR-PL) spectra of these samples were determined to study the carrier lifetimes of the catalysts (Fig. 8b). The TR-PL decay curves for these materials were analyzed using a double exponential function, and the average lifetime (Ave. τ) was calculated by the following formula [53]: τ =

(τ1 and τ2 represent the short-lived and long-lived carriers, respectively, along with their respective amplitudes A1 and A2). The average lifetime of pure Cu2O (0.11 ns) and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) (0.72 ns) are significantly longer than NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O (0.08 ns), indicating that the existed heterojunction can greatly shorten the lifetime of carriers, inhibiting their recombination. The faster decay of carriers in CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O (0.07 ns) than those single and binary photocatalysts suggest that charge recombination is suppressed, leading to improved charge separation and transfer between the catalyst interface due to the fluorescence quenching effect [54], which reveal the higher photocatalytic H2 production performance is associated with a shorter fluorescence lifetime.

Zeta potential measurement provides insights into the charged state of photocatalysts and their stability in suspension. As illustrated in Fig. 8c, the Zeta potentials for Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O are 9.43, −22.5, 18.87, and −18.39 mV, respectively. The physical mixing during catalyst preparation results in a stable interaction between Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), marked by varied Zeta potentials [55]. CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) 60/Cu2O exhibits a stronger negative charge compared to its components, suggesting increased proton (H2O) adsorption capacity conducive to the H2 evolution reaction [56]. Moreover, a higher absolute value of Zeta potential indicates stronger electrostatic repulsion, which significantly reduces particle agglomeration and sedimentation, enhancing the solution stability of the ternary composite photocatalyst [57].

The photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O and CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O under visible light were studied to verify the charge carrier separation efficiency of photocatalysts. As shown in Fig. 9 a, pure NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and Cu2O exhibit a lower photocurrent density due to the recombination of carriers in Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) during photocatalysis [58]. However, the formation of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O heterostructure improved photocurrent density, suggesting enhanced charge transfer and more reactive sites for photocatalytic reactions. Notably, the CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) 60/Cu2O exhibited the highest photocurrent density, reflecting the role of CDs as effective electronic mediators that boost visible light absorption, charge generation, and inhibit charge carrier recombination.

The EIS results were presented in Fig. 9b. A smaller Nyquist arc radius corresponds to a lower charge transfer resistance in the photocatalyst, facilitating the rapid transfer of photogenerated carriers [59]. Among all the evaluated materials, the composite CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O exhibits the smallest Nyquist arc radius, signifying the lowest interfacial resistance at the heterojunction interface. This observation suggests that the synergistic interaction between CDs, Cu2O, and NH2-MIL-125 significantly promotes the migration of photogenerated carrier [60,61]. The Mott-Schottky test was performed to determine the energy band structure of the semiconductor. Flat band potentials (Efb) of NH2-MIL-125 (−0.64 eV) and Cu2O (−0.92 eV) relative to the saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode can be known by extrapolating the intercept values obtained from the linear cross section. The linear part of Mott-Schottky diagram shows a positive slope, indicating that all the prepared materials have n-type semiconductor characteristics [62–64]. Following equation (1), the Efb relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) were calculated, which were −0.44 eV of NH2-MIL-125 and 0.72 eV of Cu2O (vs. NHE), respectively. Furthermore, combined with equations (2) and (3), and Tauc plots (Fig. 7b), the calculated valence band values (EVB) for NH2-MIL-125 and Cu2O were 1.56 eV and 2.11 eV, respectively.
(1)
E NHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.197
(2)
E CB = E fb - 0.1
(3)
E CB = E VB - E g

In order to further characterize the charge transfer process in the photocatalytic system, the CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O ternary heterojunction photocatalyst was conducted by Electron spin resonance (ESR). As shown in Fig. 10 , it can be clearly seen that CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O generated ·O2 − signals but did not produce ·OH. The generation of ·O2 − is due to the fact that the ECB of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) is more negative than O2/·O2 − (−0.33 V vs. NHE). The reason for not generating ·OH signal is that the EVB of Cu2O is smaller than the potential of ·OH/OH− (1.99 V vs. NHE), which makes Cu2O unable to generate sufficient photo-generated holes to oxidize H2O to generate ·OH [65]. Therefore, photogenerated holes (h+) transition from the VB of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) to the VB of Cu2O and oxidize TEOA to TEOA + by oxidation reaction at the VB of Cu2O, while the photogenerated electrons (e−) migrate from the CB of Cu2O to the CB of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and further migrate to the surface of electron acceptors CDs for reduction. Based on the above results analysis (XPS, CB, VB, Eg, and ESR), it can be inferred that the photocatalytic reactions occurring on CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O are consistent with a type-II charge transfer mechanism.

DFT calculations were employed to explore the electronic properties and interactions among Cu2O, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, and the band structure, density of states (DOS), and electrostatic potential. Firstly, the DFT calculation models of these materials are constructed and optimized, as shown in Fig. 11 , the original and optimized CIF files are summarized in Fig. S6. The band gap values of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and Cu2O were calculated by DFT to be approximately 2.68 eV (Fig. 12 a) and 2.39 eV (Fig. 12b), respectively, which is close to the experimental results of DRS spectroscopy. Compared with the experimental values, the calculated band gaps of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O composites (Fig. 12c and d) are narrower, which may be caused by the error caused by the discontinuous correlation function [66,67]. The DOS results in Fig. 12e–h, NH2-MIL-125 shows typical semiconductor characteristics derived from C, N, O, and Ti, and the VB electrons in NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) are susceptible to photoexcitation. The electron density of Cu atoms at CB position in NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O is lower than that of bare Cu2O, suggesting that the electron delocalization in NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O and CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O is enhanced. This means that electrons are no longer bound by Cu atoms and migrate from Cu to Ti. The electrons are retained in the NH2-MIL-125 component, with more migration paths and the conductivity of the composite photocatalyst is improved. The energy difference between vacuum and Fermi level (Ef) represents the work function [68–70]. The work function is the minimum energy required to move an electron from the Fermi level to the vacuum level [71]. The smaller the value of the work function, the easier it is for electrons in a semiconductor to migrate to other semiconductors [72–74]. Figs. 12i and j shows the electrostatic potentials of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and Cu2O, with work functions of 6.43 and 4.87 eV, respectively, indicating that NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) is a semiconductor photocatalyst with low Fermi levels and Cu2O is a semiconductor with high Fermi levels. The formation of heterojunctions and the introduction of CDs greatly reduces the work function of the catalysts to 5.45 eV, which means that electrons have a greater tendency for excitation and reaction (Fig. 12k and l). The work function values indicate that when NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) comes into contact with Cu2O to form a heterojunction interface, the electrons migrate from Cu2O nanospheres to NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) until the Ef of these two components reach equilibrium [75]. This is consistent with the XPS results.

Based on the analysis and discussion of the above experimental and DFT results, a reasonable type-II electron transfer mechanism for the photocatalytic H2 production in CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O heterojunction is illustrated in Fig. 13 . The photosensitizer EY molecules adsorbed on the surface of the photocatalyst becomes excited after absorbing energy from the visible light, forming a single excited state EY1∗. EY1∗ is unstable and transforms into a triple excited state EY3∗ through an intersystem crossing (ISC) reaction. TEOA serves as an electron donor, and EY3∗ is reduced and quenched by TEOA to form EY− (negatively charged EY). Then, EY− injects excited electrons into the CBs of Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti). Thus, EY− returns to the ground state and participates in subsequent electron transport reaction cycles. At the same time, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and Cu2O can also be excited under visible light excitation to generate the photogenerated carriers, and their electrons are transferred from VBs to CBs, thus separating the photogenerated electrons and holes. The close contact between NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) and Cu2O form type-II heterojunction, electrons in the CB of Cu2O, possessing a higher Fermi level rapidly migrate to the CB of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) with a lower Fermi level, and then these electrons can rapidly be captured by CDs (electron acceptor), resulting in an obvious electron enrichment on CDs and greatly promoting H2 evolution. Simultaneously, the photogenerated holes in VB of NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) are transferred to the VB of Cu2O by type-II heterojunction, and then consumed by TEOA, producing TEOA+. The successful construction of type-II heterojunction effectively prevents the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, CDs function as potent electron acceptors, enhancing electron migration and thereby promoting the synergistic effect among CDs, NH2-MIL-125 (Ti), and Cu2O in H2 generation. This optimized arrangement leads to improved photocatalytic efficiency and stability.

In this study, the CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O type-II heterojunction ternary photocatalyst was successfully prepared though effectively integrating CDs and NH2-MIL-125 onto Cu2O surfaces using a straightforward electrostatic self-assembly method. The synthesized ternary composite demonstrated exemplary photocatalytic activity in water splitting for H2 production, significantly outperforming Cu2O and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O configurations. The optimal formulation, CDs1/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)60/Cu2O, delivered a H2 rate of 12817.1 μmol/g/h, markedly exceeding yields from other tested compositions and highlighting the beneficial effects of the heterojunction structure. This configuration not only improves visible light absorption but also ensures more active sites are available for the photocatalytic process. The construction of type-II heterojunction in CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O facilitates efficient separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs and reduces carriers transport resistance. The synergistic effect of co-loading CDs and NH2-MIL-125 (Ti) onto Cu2O results in tight interfacial contact, which facilitates high charge migration rates. Furthermore, CDs serve dual roles as cocatalyst and electron acceptor, enhancing the interaction with water molecules due to the presence of –OH and –COOH functional groups on their surfaces. This interaction plays a critical role in promoting photocatalytic H2 evolution and suppressing electron-hole recombination, thereby enhancing the overall photocatalytic efficiency. Notably, the CDs/NH2-MIL-125 (Ti)/Cu2O photocatalysts exhibit outstanding recycling stability. This study demonstrates the effectiveness of integrating CDs into heterojunction photocatalysts, providing a promising approach for the design and synthesis of advanced photocatalytic materials for sustainable H2 production.
